food_subgroup: Noodle soup dish — yellow noodles in spicy tomato-prawn-light coconut broth with large whole prawns

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code applicable for this regional Malaysian dish preparation.

main_ingredients: large whole prawns (tiger or giant freshwater prawns)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mee udang Penang weighs approximately 520g, comprising yellow noodles (~120g), spicy tomato-prawn-coconut broth (~300g), large whole prawns (~80g), and hard-boiled egg (~20g), typically served with a wedge of lime at roadside hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database) — prawn noodle soup components; supplemented by component calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients (yellow noodles, prawns, egg, tomato, coconut cream)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single IMR MyFCD entry exists for mee udang Penang as a composite dish. Values derived from IMR MyFCD component data for yellow noodles, prawn broth, and prawns, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredient nutrient profiles. Per-100g values represent the weighted average across the full 520g bowl composition. Confidence is medium due to limited direct published data for this specific Penang preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Yellow Wheat Noodles — analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mee udang Penang. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for yellow wheat noodles (GI ~58–65, medium category). Tomato acidity and lime juice provide modest glycaemic moderation (~5–8% reduction) but the primary GL driver is 120g yellow noodles per bowl. GL of 45.1 per 520g bowl is calculated as: (GI 62 × 72.8g available carbohydrate per bowl) / 100 = 45.1. This GL is classified as very high (>20). Lime juice and tomato acidity cannot overcome the high carbohydrate load from noodles.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 2,912mg per 520g bowl, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg in a single serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mee udang Penang due to its sodium content of ~560mg per 100g (~2,912mg per bowl), which alone exceeds the WHO daily sodium limit; requesting reduced broth volume or consuming only half the broth can reduce sodium intake by approximately 40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load of ~45 per bowl driven by 120g yellow wheat noodles; medium GI of 62 combined with large carbohydrate portion produces clinically significant postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat mee udang Penang as an occasional food only, as the glycemic load of ~45 per bowl is very high; portion reduction to half a bowl (~260g) or substitution of yellow noodles with a lower-GI alternative such as glass noodles can reduce the glycemic load to a more manageable level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (2,912mg/bowl) combined with dietary cholesterol from prawns (~197mg/bowl) and saturated fat from coconut cream; tomato lycopene (4–8mg/bowl) and prawn astaxanthin provide partial antioxidant offset
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mee udang Penang to occasional consumption due to its very high sodium content and moderate cholesterol load from prawns; the tomato lycopene and prawn astaxanthin provide some antioxidant benefit but do not offset the cardiovascular risk from the sodium level, and broth consumption should be minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato lycopene (4–8mg per bowl) and prawn astaxanthin provide mild anti-inflammatory activity; sodium excess remains the primary concern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mee udang Penang contains tomato lycopene and prawn astaxanthin with mild anti-inflammatory properties that may offer modest benefit for rheumatoid arthritis, but the very high sodium content warrants caution for individuals on anti-inflammatory dietary protocols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Udang Penang is a distinctly Penang Malaysian hawker dish consisting of yellow wheat noodles served in a spicy, tangy tomato-prawn broth with light coconut cream, large whole prawns (typically tiger or giant freshwater prawns), a hard-boiled egg, and a wedge of lime. The dish is particularly associated with roadside stalls around Batu Maung, Penang. The tomato-coconut broth gives the soup a characteristic orange-red colour and a tangy, mildly creamy flavour profile that distinguishes it from the darker, pork-rib-based broth of Penang Hokkien Mee. A standard bowl weighs approximately 520g and provides around 494 kcal, 27g protein, 11.4g fat, and 72.8g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at approximately 2,912mg per bowl, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit in a single serving. The glycemic load is very high (~45 per bowl) due to the 120g yellow noodle portion. Clinically, this dish poses significant concern for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Nutritional highlights include tomato lycopene (4–8mg per bowl from cooked tomato), prawn astaxanthin (anti-inflammatory), and lime-derived Vitamin C (~14mg per bowl). The dish is halal and dairy-free. It is a popular Penang seafood hawker food also found in Malaysian restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: mee udang Penang calories per bowl

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for mee udang Penang as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for yellow wheat noodles. Medium confidence reflects the component-calculation methodology and limited direct published data for this specific Penang preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for mee udang Penang composite dish not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup dish not directly reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this preparation — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional mee udang Penang; confirmed zero per IMR MyFCD component review). Per-bowl values (520g): energy ~494 kcal, protein ~27g, fat ~11.4g, carbohydrate ~72.8g, sodium ~2,912mg. Sodium is very high — approximately 2,912mg per bowl, exceeding WHO daily limit. GL ~45 per bowl is very high. Tomato lycopene estimated at 4–8mg per bowl (cooked tomato, bioavailability enhanced). Prawn astaxanthin present. Lime Vitamin C ~14mg per bowl. Halal (no pork). Dish is a Penang regional specialty particularly associated with Batu Maung area. foodex2_code set to null: no validated FoodEx2 code has been confirmed from an authoritative FoodEx2 source for this specific Malaysian regional preparation; code omitted to preserve ontology honesty per G5.
